This capital infusion supports the development and deployment of Wavye’s next-generation AV2.0 platform.Built on Embodied AI and powered by Generative AI (GenAI) neural networks, AV2.0 vehicles are self-learning, adaptable, and vehicle-agnostic. These systems enable the AVs to process real-world sensor data in real time, allowing them to make dynamic, context-aware driving decisions without human input. While the technological advancements offer exponential gains in efficiency and scalability, consumer trust and safety remain critical. Additionally, the rise of driverless delivery services raises important ethical considerations, particularly regarding potential job displacement in the gig economy—a sector already marked by economic uncertainty.As the industry moves toward greater autonomy, balancing innovation with social responsibility will be essential for long-term success and public acceptance.Singapore stands out as a global leader in GenAI deployment. Under its Smart Nation 2.0 and National AI Strategy 2.0 (NAIS 2.0), the city-state is leveraging GenAI to drive innovation and improve public services. Notably, its "Virtual Singapore" digital twin uses GenAI to simulate scenarios for urban planning, disaster preparedness, and risk management.The government is also partnering with major tech players—such as Google through AI sandboxes and Microsoft via the AI Pinnacle Program—to explore GenAI applications in finance and beyond. These initiatives aim to ensure that GenAI benefits Singapore’s residents while mitigating associated risks. Efforts like Job Transformation Maps are being developed to help workers reskill and stay relevant in a changing job market.With strong leadership from the public sector, Singapore is paving the way for the private sector to explore new business models and revenue opportunities built on GenAI.Consumer expectations for greater personalisation continue to grow, with the most digitally savvy audiences demanding richer, more tailored experiences throughout their customer journey. In response, retailers, brands, and hospitality operators are increasingly leveraging Generative AI (GenAI) to enhance interactions, deliver smarter recommendations, and offer highly personalised assistance.In today’s competitive market, GenAI empowers businesses to elevate customer interactions from merely functional to genuinely memorable—building deeper relationships and fostering stronger brand loyalty.GenAI is transforming the marketing landscape by enabling:Importantly, GenAI is also enhancing creativity in product ideation and development. It supports the optimisation and scalable measurement of content performance, driving both innovation and impact.As adoption increases, performance-oriented marketing metrics will evolve. Human oversight will remain critical to ensure quality control, copyright compliance, and brand consistency. For smaller businesses, a more measured approach—adopting GenAI on a manageable scale—can help test its value and compatibility with internal operations.GenAI is no longer a fringe innovation, it is reshaping the commercial and creative landscape. By redefining value, accelerating innovation, transforming product development, and enhancing service delivery, GenAI presents an unprecedented opportunity for growth.The brands embracing GenAI aren’t just keeping pace—they’re setting the standard for a more intelligent, engaging, and profitable future. For those yet to begin, the message is clear: adapt now or risk being left behind.So, whether your organisation thought to execute the Generative Artificial Intelligence to empower businesses and to elevate customer interactions from merely functional to genuinely memorable—building deeper relationships and fostering stronger brand loyalty?(The Author is Professor, Trainer, and Consultant in Marketing at Lal Bahadur Shastri Institute of Management, New Delhi, India. The views expressed are personal.)

title: In AI Race, Ethics Collateral Damage?
article_text: There is a troubling ethical dimension to the development. Typically, lawmakers get to decide the limits of technology development, or its dispersal. But the law is still some way behind AI's development. The ethical burden, thus, must be shared by producers and consumers until voters decide on the matter. This was how human cloning for reproductive purposes was halted long before it was widely banned. Tech companies share reservations over AI safeguards. But these may not withstand the competitive intensity of strong investor interest. So, there may be a case for allowing the law to catch up with AI.The US approach to regulating AI has been through a conversation with technology creators. Any ethical concerns emerging from the other side of the table acquire special emphasis in dialogue-based rule-setting. Unlike earlier technology developments, the state has a diminished role in the evolution of AI. Tech creators must be comfortable with the products they are bringing to the market. They need to be sure automation can fulfil customer expectations. Hopefully, Anthropic will find a resolution to its moral dilemma in court. The incident is, of course, a chilling reminder of the intensity of the AI race, where ethics can very easily become collateral damage.

title: India AI Impact Summit 2026: Google maps the playbook for India’s AI builders
article_text: At the ongoing India AI Impact Summit 2026, leaders from Google DeepMind Google Cloud, Android and Google Play convened developers, founders and ecosystem partners to discuss how the intelligence revolution is reshaping product development, platforms and participation across India’s fast growing digital economy.Senior executives from the tech giant observed that artificial intelligence has moved beyond experimentation and proof of concept. The next phase, they said, is about scaling responsibly, building full stack capabilities and enabling wider participation from India’s developer community.Anand Rangarajan, VP, engineering at Google DeepMind , traced the arc of technological progress over the past three decades. “Technology first expanded access to information. Then it enabled people to learn faster. After that, it drove productivity,” he said. “With generative AI, creativity has become the next frontier.” Rangarajan outlined three core focus areas: expanding human capability, widening access and deepening participation.On capability, he noted that each wave of technology has amplified what individuals and businesses can do. Generative AI, he argued, goes a step further by unlocking creative potential at scale. On access, he said the key question is how to ensure these capabilities are available seamlessly across platforms and devices.Participation, however, was the central theme of his address. “How do we enable others to be part of this revolution? How do we help them innovate and grow?” he asked.Placing this shift in historical context, Rangarajan described four waves: the computing revolution of the 1990s, the information revolution of the 2000s, the mobile and cloud revolution of the 2010s, and the intelligence revolution from 2020 onwards.“Earlier shifts changed specific aspects of business, whether it was distribution, computing location or customer engagement,” he said. “AI is transforming everything at once, from product development to supply chains to collaboration.”He cited examples of startups reaching $100 million in annual recurring revenue within 12 months with teams of around 10 people. “Small teams can now create outsized impact, provided they leverage AI effectively,” he said.Rangarajan also highlighted ecosystem initiatives including Google Developer Groups, which number more than 1,000 globally including around 70 in India, and 93 Google Developer Experts in the country. Through programmes such as Build with AI and the Google for StartupsAccelerator, which has supported over 1,700 alumni globally who have collectively raised more than $30 billion and created more than 110,000 jobs, Google aims to widen participation. Applications for the current accelerator cohort are open until April 19.Prashanth Subrahmanyam , lead developer adoption, APAC at Google Cloud, framed his remarks around a central question: in a world of AI powered prototyping, what does it take to move from building an application to scaling it, sharing it and growing a sustainable user base?“We are at a unique moment where AI is mature, accessible and affordable,” he said. “The pace of progress compared to even a year ago is extraordinary.”He observed that traditional boundaries between front end developers, back end engineers, designers and product managers are dissolving. “With AI tools, product managers can prototype independently, and developers can better understand markets and user segments. Personas are merging,” he said.Developers, he added, are increasingly acting as managers of AI agents. “Rather than writing every line of code manually, they orchestrate multiple agents, direct tasks, review outputs and refine results. Managing agents is becoming as important as writing code.”While product cycles have compressed dramatically, from months to weeks or even minutes for early prototypes, Subrahmanyam cautioned that fundamentals remain unchanged. “Planning still matters. Security, scalability and reliability remain essential. Quality cannot be generated with a single prompt,” he said. “AI changes the how, not always the what.”He outlined an AI powered product lifecycle spanning strategy, discovery and delivery. Tools such as Gemini Deep Research assist with analysing reports and market opportunities, while NotebookLM enables teams to upload research and extract insights.“Data is the true differentiator in AI applications,” he said. “Strong databases, warehouses and analytics pipelines are foundational to building intelligent systems at scale.”Concluding, he urged developers to experiment boldly while adopting integrated full stack platforms. “In this era, the speed of learning may matter more than anything else.”Amrit Sanjeev, staff developer relations engineer for Android at Google, focused on how user expectations have fundamentally shifted in the AI era. “Users no longer want reactive apps,” he said. “They expect intelligent, personalised and proactive experiences that anticipate their needs.”He grouped Android AI use cases into three categories: helpful experiences such as proactive fraud detection and contextual assistance; content experiences including summarisation, rewriting and classification; and social and creative experiences such as image generation and contextual editing.On device inference offers strong privacy and offline capability without per request inference costs, while cloud models provide larger context windows and broader modality support. “There is no one size fits all answer. Architecture must align with use case and regulatory constraints,” he said.He also encouraged developers to explore advanced techniques such as function calling, retrieval augmented generation and fine tuning to build adaptive AI workflows. “The spectrum of AI capabilities available to Android developers today is unprecedented. The opportunity lies in experimenting quickly and delivering intelligent mobile experiences.”Aneesha Dhar, head India Ecosystem at Google Play, underscored the scale of the opportunity in India, with nearly 600 million users on the Play Store. Around 16.7 per cent of users of India-developed apps and games come from outside the country, highlighting global reach.“Google Play is not just a marketplace. It is a launchpad where apps are discovered, scaled and turned into sustainable businesses,” she said.She outlined four pillars for developers: acquire, grow, engage deeply and monetise.App quality, she stressed, directly affects discoverability and business continuity. Using tools such as Android Vitals, developers can monitor crash rates, application not responding errors and other stability metrics. “Forty four per cent of one star reviews cite bugs and stability issues. Seventy three per cent of five star reviews mention speed, design and usability. Performance directly influences ratings, and ratings influence growth,” she said.She also referenced the internal six megabyte rule, noting that every six megabyte increase in APK size can reduce install conversion rates by roughly one per cent. “App size matters more than many developers realise,” she said.To strengthen store presence, Google Play offers Store Listing Experiments for A B testing of icons, screenshots and descriptions, and Custom Store Listings with up to 50 variations tailored to specific audiences and markets.On monetisation, Dhar highlighted free trials, introductory and localised pricing for acquisition, along with grace periods and subscription pause features to reduce involuntary churn. Price Experiments on Play Console allow developers to test multiple pricing tiers before making permanent changes.“Do not guess your pricing strategy. Test it,” she said. “Structured experiments often reveal that certain user segments are willing to pay more than assumed.”As AI increasingly becomes mainstream and is often first experienced through mobile applications, Dhar argued that distribution, quality and monetisation discipline will determine which products scale sustainably.“For developers building in the intelligence revolution, the opportunity is immense. With the right fundamentals, Indian apps can win not just at home, but globally,” she concluded.

title: India AI Impact Summit 2026: Governing AI at billion-scale
article_text: The responsible adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) at scale is increasingly shaping economic growth, public trust and digital resilience . As AI systems expand across sectors, attention is turning to how governance frameworks, content integrity safeguards and cyber readiness can be embedded from the outset. The focus is on how safety, transparency and accountability can evolve alongside innovation, and how inclusive, adaptable approaches to AI governance can support social good while strengthening resilience in rapidly changing environments.At the ongoing India AI Impact Summit 2026 , a session brought together Dr. Subi Chaturvedi, SVP and chief corporate affairs and public officer, InMobi; Carly Ramsey, head of public policy, Asia Pacific, Japan and China, Cloudflare; Jay Bavisi, president, EC-Council; Anna Golovkina, associate professor, Saint Petersburg State University; Beenu Arora, co-founder & CEO, Cyble; Lt General (Dr) Rajesh Pant and Dr. Suresh Yadav, director of Climate Change and Oceans Directorate, Commonwealth Secretariat, who discussed how responsible AI must move beyond intent and into execution. The discussion was moderated by Vineet Kumar, founder and president, CyberPeace Foundation.Chaturvedi spoke about India’s scale and complexity. “In India, we build for millions and sometimes billions. Responsible AI cannot be a checklist, it has to work at population scale,” she said.She added that governance models shaped in Western regulatory environments cannot simply be replicated. “We are not only solving for privacy. We are solving for access, multilingual inclusion and digital literacy. Transparency and accountability have to grow alongside innovation,” she said, emphasising that trust remains central to adoption.Ramsey turned the focus to cybersecurity architecture. “AI is accelerating the threat landscape. We are seeing attacks that are more automated, more targeted and happening at unprecedented scale,” she said. “Security cannot be something we add later. AI systems have to be secure by design. And if every country goes its own way on standards, innovation will slow and complexity will rise,” she added.Bavisi highlighted a deeper structural challenge capability. Frameworks already exist, from the National Institute of Standards and Technology to international ISO standards, but implementation capacity is still catching up, he said.He added that technology alone will not determine outcomes. “AI versus AI is already happening in cybersecurity. But the real pivot point is the human being. Building skills and capability is no longer optional, it’s essential,” noted Bavisi.Dr. Yadav closed with urgency. “The next two years will define the next two decades for India’s AI journey,” he said.He added that responsible AI must rest on five foundations. “Safety, security, integrity, accountability and inclusiveness have to move together. This cannot be done by one sector alone; government, industry, academia and civil society all have a role,” he said.AI’s expansion is no longer merely a technology story. It is a story about governance, resilience and trust and whether institutions can evolve at the pace innovation demands. The challenge is not simply adopting new tools, but ensuring that legal frameworks, regulatory systems and public institutions mature alongside them. Countries and organisations that invest in institutional readiness now, updating policy, strengthening oversight and building skills early, will be better positioned to harness AI’s long-term value rather than manage its unintended consequences.

title: Tech companies using content without consent: IPA prez Karine Pansa on AI-generated material
article_text: Tech companies sign accord to combat AI-generated election trickery Tech executives from Adobe, Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI and TikTok gathered at the Munich Security Conference to announce a new voluntary framework for how they will respond to AI-generated deepfakes that deliberately trick voters. See More Details The International Publishers' Association ( IPA ) president Karine Pansa accused big tech nology companies of using copyrighted content "without permission" for AI-generated materials and urged publishers to raise their concerns. Pansa, who recently visited India for the ongoing New Delhi World Book Fair, said the IPA is holding discussions with its member countries, including India, on how to deal with the big tech companies for infringing publishers' copyrights by "reproducing their materials without permission" for AI training."The big tech companies are using the content available on the internet to regenerate the new content without consent, and part of this content is books, the content of which belongs to the publishers. It is being used without their permission, consent, licensed payment, and remuneration."So it is an important part of the IPA's job to discuss how each country is going to deal with this new technology and this new copyright infringement," Pansa told PTI, adding that the organisation will soon pen an open letter regarding the issue.IPA, founded in 1896 in Paris with the promotion and defence of copyright and the freedom to publish, is the world's largest federation of national, regional and specialist publishers' associations.Based in Geneva, Switzerland, the organisation comprises 100 plus members from 81 countries.Terming the alleged copyright infringement by the big tech companies -- including Microsoft Meta and OpenAI -- a serious matter, Pansa said it is sad that not many people, even those belonging to the publishing industry, are aware of the unfair use of the authors' copyrighted works.She encouraged publishers to come forward and raise their concerns, ensuring IPA's full support."We need to make sure that the publishers also value their own work, because sometimes they don't."They just publish, they do their best with their content, but they do not brand, they don't value their names, we need to make them powerful, just the same way as these tech companies have," pointed Pansa, who is also the owner of Girassol Brasil Edicoes -- a children's book publishing house in Sao Paolo, Brazil.Globally, several groups of authors, including the likes of novelist John Grisham and "Game of Thrones" author George RR Martin, have filed lawsuits over the use of their text in AI training.Elaborating on how to deal with generative AI content , the Brazilian publisher argued against amending copyright laws and said the current set of rules are well-equipped to hold the big tech companies accountable for the violation."The law doesn't need to be changed or updated because it is clearly mentioned what is fair use and what is unfair. So, it doesn't need to be changed , it just needs to be respected," she added.That said, Pansa here clarified that she is not someone who is against the use of AI per se and in fact encourages authors to embrace the technology.When asked about concerns regarding writers' running out of jobs because of the advent of AI, she replied: "AI is not creative enough, it is just recombination of used patterns and data at best"."We are not asking to end GPT . We all should embrace technology, and authors today are using it, maybe to correct their draft or to make sure that they have the right words. But the main idea, the original creation, is theirs," she concluded.

title: The rise of AI-generated drivel on LinkedIn
article_text: If you've browsed LinkedIn lately, you may have noticed an uptick in posts that sound vaguely inspirational and insightful yet ultimately say little of substance. The culprit? Artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT that enable users to auto-generate fluffy yet professional-sounding posts with minimal effort.Over the past year, AI writing tools have exploded in popularity as they've become more accessible and easier to use. With a few simple prompts, these tools can churn out entire articles and social media posts that, on the surface, read like they could have been written by a human.However, much of this AI-generated content , especially on LinkedIn , tends to follow a formulaic style – an upbeat tone, liberal use of buzzwords, and generic insights that resonate with a broad audience but lack original ideas. Posts often rely on vague platitudes about leadership, success, and innovation without digging into the actual "how" or sharing real examples.This type of content, while inoffensive and mildly engaging, has been dubbed "genai" (generated AI) due to its unmistakably bland style. Genai posts are usually easy to spot once you know what to look for. They go heavy on jargon and filler statements while being light on novel concepts. The end result reads like a mashup of motivational posters and business school essays.The proliferation of Gen AI on LinkedIn is starting to have a numbing effect, cluttering feeds with vapid posts that all blur together. As more people realize how easy it is to generate passable LinkedIn content with AI, the volume of Gen AI will likely keep increasing. LinkedIn has always had its fair share of self-promotion and hype, but AI is allowing that superficial content to scale.To be clear, AI writing tools are not inherently bad. They can be quite helpful for brainstorming, outlining, and overcoming writer's block. The problem arises when people grow overly reliant on AI to the point that authenticity, expertise and original thinking get lost.I took a look at this LinkedIn post shared, which provides advice on how businesses can leverage AI and IoT technologies . I live in this space day in day out.While I understand the intention behind the post, I have to respectfully disagree with some of its recommendations and implications. The risk is all too real that I end up in a conversation with a business or regulatory leader that may actually take this seriously as a good idea, it's not, at least for IoT! . Here's why I think this post exemplifies the problematic aspects of generic AI-generated advice on LinkedIn:- Terrible advice takes the form of broad statements about the potential of AI and IoT without providing concrete examples or use cases. It throws out buzzwords like "smart tech" and "digital transformation" without explaining what those actually entail for businesses. The advice is so generic that it could apply to virtually any industry, which limits its practical value.-Implementing AI and IoT is not as simple as the post suggests. It sounds like businesses just need to adopt these technologies and success will follow. In reality, leveraging AI and IoT requires significant planning, investment, and expertise. Businesses need to carefully consider how these tools fit into their specific strategies and operations. Any one who has been in the trenches of emerging tech innovation knows that glossing over these important nuances makes it incredibly hard to make progress.Leaning heavily into the hype around AI and IoT without acknowledging the challenges and limitations of these technologies. It presents them as cure-alls that will automatically lead to "improved efficiency, reduced costs, and enhanced customer experiences." While AI and IoT certainly have the potential to drive value, the post fails to mention the significant work required to realize those benefits or the potential risks and drawbacks. The counterintuitive nature of emerging tech Like AI, IoT & 5g is that even product people present it as a solution first than starting at the problem space to evaluate how these technologies could be a good fit.Perhaps most tellingly, there's nothing particularly original or insightful in this post. Its recommendations echo common refrains about digital transformation that have been repeated ad nauseam. The post doesn't draw on the author's unique expertise or experiences. Instead, it rehashes surface-level talking points, likely pulled from other sources via AI.In my experience, too small piecemeal approach to IoT is not the right strategy for half the cases, this is because the lifecycle for an IoT project to show results is 11 to 40 months. Because IoT often means to effect change at operational level and change is hard. Corporate entrepreneurship graveyards are filled with IoT initiatives that fell off after the pilot and even a successful early adopter traction.My personal pet peeve, the Impersonal tone- The writing style is stiff and impersonal, lacking any sense of the author's unique voice or perspective. The post reads like it was stitched together from generic snippets rather than flowing naturally from the author's own thoughts. This bland, artificial tone is a telltale sign of AI-generated content The good intent of the author is commendable, and I appreciate & empathise with the drive to create content and raise awareness outside their area of comfort. While it touches on important topics, it ultimately falls short of providing meaningful, actionable advice. Its generic recommendations and lack of depth exemplify the limitations of relying too heavily on AI to generate thought leadership content.For LinkedIn to fulfill its potential as a platform for valuable professional insights, we need more posts that draw on the author's genuine expertise and experiences, not just regurgitate common talking points. AI can be a helpful tool, but it's no substitute for original thinking and authentic expression.As a platform designed for professional networking and discourse, LinkedIn needs more than just inoffensive fluff pieces. We should strive to share insights drawn from actual experience, showcase real accomplishments, and dare to voice unique perspectives. That's harder than plugging some prompts into an AI, but it's how we'll elevate the conversation and get value from our LinkedIn connections.So the next time you see a LinkedIn post filled with generic platitudes about "synergy" and "disruption," chances are it's Gen AI . Don't be fooled by the shiny packaging - look for posts where the author's knowledge and personality shine through. Let's bring more authenticity back to LinkedIn, even if we can't stop the oncoming flood of Gen AI completely.

title: The future of brand storytelling - How AI-generated content is revolutionizing marketing
article_text: The ingenious world of Marketing finds itself bracing a novel frontier where artificial intelligence continues to transform traditional methods at an extraordinary pace. Conventional approaches to brand messaging are rapidly evolving as AI technologies have changed how companies connect with their audiences. This ubiquitous shift represents more than a technological advancement; it signals a fundamental reimagining of brand storytelling capabilities.Since its mainstream emergence, the marketing landscape has espoused a host of sophisticated AI-powered narrative strategies that have the power to capture audience attention and build lasting connections. The present-day consumer seeks authentic brand experiences that can resonate on a personal level. Thus, storytelling is the cornerstone of effective marketing because it creates emotional bonds that drive brand loyalty and measurable business outcomes. Artificial intelligence now amplifies these storytelling capabilities by offering unprecedented opportunities for brands to craft compelling narratives at scale.AI technologies have truly revolutionized content creation through their advanced personalization techniques. By analyzing vast amounts of user data, AI systems identify individual preferences, behaviors, and engagement patterns, enabling marketers to deliver tailored content that speaks directly to specific audience cohorts. This level of personalization transforms generic marketing into meaningful conversations that drive engagement and conversion.The dynamic storytelling capabilities of AI represent another breakthrough for marketing teams. These technologies adapt messaging in real time based on user interactions to create responsive campaigns that evolve with audience engagement. This fluidity allows brands to maintain relevance throughout the customer journey, addressing specific needs and interests as they emerge. The entire process improves the relevance of the communication and ensures brands do not have to deal with media spend spillovers.Content generation has been streamlined through sophisticated AI tools that produce blogs, advertisements, and social media posts with remarkable efficiency. These systems analyze successful content patterns and replicate effective approaches while introducing creative variations that keep messaging fresh and engaging. The result is consistent, high-quality content without the traditional resource demands.Perhaps most impressive is AI's contribution to video content production. Numerous platforms like Runway ML, Midjourney , and Sora now generate professional-quality digital video commercials without traditional production teams. These AI systems can create full-fledged cinematic advertisements that effectively reduce weeks of production time to mere hours while maintaining that which is most essential: visual excellence and narrative coherence. In the realm of brand marketing, AI brings an unprecedented level of transformation, not just reducing the turnaround times but saving crucial marketing dollars spent, ensuring the best ROIs. Conversational AI has transformed customer interactions through intelligent chatbots and virtual assistants that deliver personalized brand experiences. These systems maintain a consistent brand voice while addressing individual customer inquiries, creating seamless storytelling touchpoints across all customer interactions.AI technologies enable brands to scale storytelling efforts without sacrificing quality or authenticity. Marketing teams can now produce customized content for diverse audience segments simultaneously, maintaining consistent brand messaging while addressing unique audience interests and needs.The real-time personalization capabilities of AI systems create dynamic content experiences that adapt to individual user behaviors and preferences. This responsiveness dramatically improves engagement metrics and strengthens audience connections through content that evolves with each interaction.Creative processes benefit tremendously from AI collaboration, as these systems generate novel concepts, design elements, and narrative approaches that expand creative possibilities. While human creativity remains essential, AI augmentation introduces unexpected innovations that enrich brand storytelling Advanced analytics provided by AI platforms offer unprecedented insight into campaign performance. These systems identify content elements that drive engagement, allowing marketers to refine storytelling approaches based on concrete performance data rather than subjective assessments.Strategic decisions now leverage comprehensive data analysis that reveals audience preferences, content effectiveness, and emerging trends. This intelligence allows marketing teams to develop storytelling strategies grounded in verifiable insights rather than assumptions.Marketing teams must develop familiarity with available AI technologies to make informed implementation decisions. The growing ecosystem includes content generation platforms, analytics systems, personalization engines, and video production tools, each offering unique capabilities that enhance brand storytelling.Strategic integration requires thoughtful planning to align AI capabilities with brand objectives. Successful implementation begins with identifying specific storytelling challenges that AI can address, followed by careful technology selection and measured deployment that balances innovation with established brand values.Industry leaders continue to push boundaries in AI marketing applications, exploring emerging technologies like synthetic actors, AI-generated voiceovers, and hyper-personalized visual content. Brands that monitor these developments gain a competitive advantage through early adoption of breakthrough storytelling techniques.Ethical considerations remain paramount as brands integrate AI into storytelling strategies. Maintaining transparency about AI usage builds audience trust, while balancing algorithmic efficiency with human creativity ensures brand narratives retain authentic emotional resonance that purely synthetic content might lack.Artificial intelligence has fundamentally transformed brand storytelling capabilities, creating unprecedented opportunities for personalized, dynamic, and engaging marketing content. The brands that thrive in this new environment will be those that embrace AI as a collaborative partner in the creative process rather than a replacement for human insight.The future belongs to marketing teams that skillfully blend AI efficiency with human creativity, leveraging technological capabilities while maintaining an authentic brand voice. As AI tools continue to evolve, the possibilities for innovative storytelling will expand, creating new opportunities for meaningful brand connections.The transformation ensues with full steam ahead.. Forward-thinking marketers are implementing AI storytelling strategies today in their bid to gain valuable experience and a competitive advantage in this rapidly evolving landscape. The question is no longer whether AI will change brand storytelling but how quickly marketers will adapt to this powerful new reality.(The author is an Assistant Vice President (AVP) - brand at Olyv.)

title: Opinion: Canadians deserve trusted news — Not AI-generated noise
article_text: When Meta instituted its Canadian news ban in August 2023 in response to the Online News Act, CHEK’s Facebook and Instagram accounts effectively went dark. Years of building trust, building audience, and building community were wiped out with the flip of a switch. At the time, Meta said it was removing all news from its platforms in Canada. But here’s what Canadians are seeing today: news-like content still circulates widely on Facebook and Instagram — just not from trusted, accountable Canadian newsrooms. Trusted, fact-checked journalism produced by local newsrooms like CHEK is blocked — while misleading or AI-generated content designed to mimic real reporting is allowed to spread. Blocking trusted journalism does not eliminate news from social media. It simply removes credible sources and creates space for misinformation to flourish. We believe Facebook and Instagram users in Canada should have access to trusted local news — not just viral content designed to provoke outrage or generate clicks.

title: Contextual intelligence becomes the new standard for exceptional customer experience in 2026
article_text: Zendesk has released its 2026 Customer Experience Trends report, examining how the growing use of artificial intelligence alongside data and human judgment is shaping customer service expectations.The report notes that consumer expectations in India continue to rise. Around 80% of Indian consumers say their service standards have increased over the past year, while 79% expect more personalised experiences supported by AI. At the same time, 96% want clear explanations for AI-driven decisions. Despite this, fewer than half of organisations currently provide a fully traceable or auditable record of how AI decisions are made, and a similar share of customer service agents consider trust and transparency to be a top priority.Business leaders acknowledge these gaps. More than 90% of respondents believe disconnected data threatens consistent service delivery, and a similar proportion say customers are likely to abandon brands that cannot resolve issues during the first interaction. In response, nearly half of Indian organisations have adopted prompt-based analytics tools, a number expected to rise significantly within the next year. Most are also introducing AI reasoning controls to improve oversight and accountability.Based on input from more than 11,000 consumers, customer experience leaders, and service agents worldwide, the report finds that organisations are increasingly combining automation with human decision-making to improve service outcomes.Consumers are placing greater value on continuity across interactions. In India, 83% want agents to retain context across channels, while 72% report frustration when required to repeat information. Most CX leaders agree that maintaining context across conversations strengthens relationships and reduces friction.Speed alone is no longer sufficient for customer satisfaction. Nearly 90% of Indian consumers say both response time and issue resolution influence purchasing decisions, and most expect service speeds to match or exceed those of the previous year. CX leaders widely believe that failure to resolve issues quickly will lead to customer loss.Customer engagement is also shifting toward multimodal interactions, with users expecting to move seamlessly between text, voice, and visual formats within a single conversation. A majority of Indian consumers prefer support models that allow this flexibility, and CX leaders increasingly view voice-based AI as an important factor in future service delivery.The report also highlights growing use of AI-driven analytics to support faster decision-making. While adoption in India is increasing rapidly, many organisations still cite a lack of common standards as a challenge.Transparency in AI systems is becoming a central concern. While most consumers expect clear explanations for AI-generated outcomes, many organisations have yet to implement systems that provide clear and auditable decision trails. Many are now taking steps to address this gap through stronger governance and reasoning controls.The report is based on surveys conducted in June 2025 across 22 countries, including India, involving more than 11,000 respondents comprising consumers, customer experience leaders, and service agents.

title: AI as the new OS: Can enterprises trust a thinking machine?
article_text: Artificial Intelligence has long been touted as a transformative force, but the discussion at the ETCIO Annual Conclave 2025 pushed that narrative into more provocative territory: Is AI becoming the cognitive operating system of the enterprise—and can we trust it to think?Moderated by Shipra Malhotra, the session brought together Navendu Agarwal, Group CIO, Ola; Shuchi Mahajan, SVP & Head - Fraud Prevention, Analytics, Digital & Customer Awareness, HDFC Bank; Binit Jha, CDO, IDBI Bank, for a candid conversation on what happens when AI becomes not just an assistant, but the actual decision-making engine of the enterprise. From explainability and regulatory friction to cultural shifts and cognitive accountability, the panel explored the real-world implications of enterprise-wide AI adoption.Navendu Agarwal offered a compelling metaphor to frame the shift: “AI is no longer a tool—it’s the second brain of the enterprise.” Previously, dashboards and BI tools supported decision-making. Today, AI does the heavy lifting of assimilating data across systems and summarizing it contextually, empowering decision-makers directly. This shift redefines agility: enterprise leaders no longer have to depend on layers of interpretation—they can interface with AI directly to derive business value.Agarwal argued that generative models have fundamentally altered the nature of enterprise intelligence. LLMs can now analyze and interpret massive data volumes without needing task-specific training. “It takes the power away from technologists and gives it back to decision-makers,” he said.In the highly regulated world of banking, Binit Jha described AI’s rapid evolution as both a boon and a bottleneck. “AI moves every 24 hours. Banks and regulators don’t,” he quipped. For institutions like IDBI Bank—both regulated and a public-sector entity—the challenge lies in matching AI’s pace within the constraints of compliance.Yet Jha emphasized that AI isn’t optional. The bank is categorizing use cases based on risk and regulatory clarity to accelerate safe implementation. “We have to implement it anyway,” he said. The key is to identify areas where AI can operate within existing guardrails—like analytics, scoring, and workflow automation—while human oversight governs final decisions.A 2024 PwC India study cited during the session revealed a trust gap: 73% of Indian enterprises trust AI for operational decisions, but only 28% trust it for strategic or financial ones. The difference? Explainability.Shuchi Mahajan emphasized that while AI augments speed and efficiency—especially in fraud analytics and customer scoring—it cannot replace human judgment where empathy, ethics, and reputational risk are involved. “Cognitive accountability will become the soul of this new OS,” she said. The future belongs to organizations that embed trust as a cultural cornerstone.She advocated for a 360-degree trust model that begins with employees. As AI introduces uncertainty about job security, Mahajan stressed the need for leaders to upskill teams not in coding, but in applied AI fluency. “People must know how to prompt and use AI to solve problems—not build it,” she noted. That shift requires cultural transformation as much as technical modernization.Both Mahajan and Jha outlined clear boundaries where AI should never operate autonomously:Navendu Agarwal added the nuance: “AI should never replace cognitive work—it should accelerate it.” He underscored that decision-making must stay human unless it's entirely predictable and rule-based. “AI is your intern—it delivers. But interpretation is your job.”Looking five years ahead, the panel explored what strategic shifts CXOs must undergo. Mahajan asserted that tech-first leadership must evolve into trust-building leadership—across employees, partners, regulators, and customers.Navendu Agarwal agreed but reframed AI as an accelerator, not a replacement. “Technology becomes more accessible, less intimidating. You don’t need a tech PhD to lead transformation—you need curiosity and clarity,” he said. This will democratize innovation, enable faster decision-making, and redefine the CXO role as strategic orchestrator rather than operational executor.The session concluded with a powerful truth: AI may think, but humans remain responsible. As Mahajan put it, “If AI is the OS, accountability is the conscience.” Success will belong to enterprises that view AI not as a plug-and-play solution, but as a deeply integrated partner—governed by values, ethics, and strategic clarity.AI is no longer just a tool—it’s becoming the enterprise’s cognitive core. But trust, governance, and strategic leadership are non-negotiable. Enterprises must move from AI adoption to AI accountability—balancing speed with ethics, and intelligence with empathy.

title: Industry consultations on mandatory labelling of AI-generated content over, rules soon: S Krishnan
article_text: The government has completed consultations with the industry on the proposed mandatory labelling of AI-generated content, and the related rules are set to be issued shortly, IT Secretary S Krishnan has said.Krishnan told PTI in an interview that the industry has been "f ai rly responsible", understands the logic behind labelling of AI content and that, as such, there has not been serious pushback against it.The primary feedback from the industry is around wanting clarity on the degree of modification to make clear distinctions between substantive, material changes through AI, and routine technical enhancements."Based on the inputs we have received, we are just consulting the other ministries within government, saying that these are the changes which have been suggested... so which changes we accept, which changes we make and what tweaks we make... that portion is on right now, and I think we should come out with the new rules very shortly," Krishnan said.About the feedback and inputs coming in from the industry on this, Krishnan said, "I don't think they are against it.""And again, this is not something which we are either asking them to register or go to a third party entity or placing some restriction of any kind. All that is being asked is label the content," he said, asserting that citizens have the right to know whether a particular piece of content has been generated synthetically or is authentic.Krishnan said, at times, even minor AI edits may significantly alter meanings, while routine technical enhancements, say smartphone's camera related improvements, may enhance quality without changing facts."Most of the reaction is the degree and what kind of change because now advanced technology is such that there is some modification or the other in some sense. In some cases, modification can be very small, but that, in itself, can make a difference... One word in a sentence can make a huge difference to what the outcome is," he explained.As such, use of technology and modern devices involve some level of enhancements."...the way that you photograph or take a video or record something, the phone itself enhances some of this, tries to make it better. So they (industry) want some clarity that those kind of technical changes, which don't alter anything in substance, but they are enhancements, are not simultaneously called into question when you do something like this... I think those kind of reasonable asks, we can certainly accommodate," Krishnan said.That said, excluding all kinds of modifications can be an issue."Because, as I pointed out, even one or two words changing in a particular sequence of conversation could have a completely different effect and impact... say, the rest of it is a real conversation, but you used AI to change two or three words in a particular sequence of things that somebody says, it can make all the difference. And creativity also has its place and we are not against creativity, but people have a right to know this is actual real stuff, and this is not real," he said.In October, the government had proposed changes to IT rules, mandating the clear labelling of AI-generated content and increasing the accountability of large platforms like Facebook and YouTube for verifying and flagging synthetic information, to curb user harm from deepfakes and misinformation.The IT ministry noted that deepfake audio, videos, and synthetic media going viral on social platforms have demonstrated the potential of generative AI to create "convincing falsehoods", where such content can be "weaponised" to spread misinformation, damage reputations, manipulate or influence elections, or commit financial fraud.The proposed amendments to IT rules provide a clear legal basis for labelling, traceability, and accountability related to synthetically-generated information, the ministry had said.The ministry had invited comments from stakeholders on the draft amendment mandating labelling, visibility, and metadata embedding for synthetically generated or modified information to distinguish such content from authentic media.The draft rules involved mandating platforms to label AI-generated content with prominent markers and identifiers, covering a minimum of 10 per cent of the visual display or the initial 10 per cent of the duration of an audio clip.

title: Beyond the Black Box: Why AI Needs Transparency and Explainability
article_text: Gone are the days when AI was considered a futuristic concept. Today, it has been deeply embedded in our day-to-day activities, shaping most of our decisions - from financial transactions to critical health diagnostics. Moving from merely employing AI for certain tasks to becoming AI-first organizations has been crucial for businesses to harvest its value at scale. As our dependency on AI for decision-making increases, a significant underlying challenge arises: the transparency and explainability of AI, which we can describe as the “black box” problem. This black box nature of AI stems from its complexity. Many AI models are built as opaque systems, producing decisions without clear explanations. Despite their high accuracy, AI models often operate opaquely, hiding their decision-making processes. This lack of transparency fuels distrust and raises concerns around ethics and fairness. The solution? Move from a black box to a glass box approach, providing visibility into AI systems while prioritizing transparency and explainability to ensure responsible AI use. Glass-box AI models are designed to explain their decisions clearly, making them more understandable to users. When users understand how AI reaches decisions, they are more likely to trust the outcomes. Another crucial pillar of Responsible AI is fairness. Bias is a well-documented issue with AI models, and generative AI is not immune. AI doesn’t create bias on its own, but learns from patterns in existing data. A model might associate specific traits with a certain gender or ethnicity, leading to inaccurate predictions and offensive outcomes. In a black box scenario, such biases may go unnoticed. With a glass box approach, biases can be detected, analyzed, and corrected. Transparent AI enables users to challenge and refine AI-driven decisions, reducing blind reliance on technology. This ability is essential in industries like healthcare and finance, where unchecked errors or biases could lead to severe consequences. Open and explainable AI fosters interdisciplinary collaboration between engineers, ethicists, and policymakers. Development and deployment should not be limited to data scientists alone. History offers a cautionary tale. Just like the nuclear arms race escalated without early regulations, AI development is accelerating. Without proactive governance, we might risk unforeseen consequences. Embedding responsible AI from the outset ensures its growth is guided by responsibility rather than blind ambition.

title: How AI is redefining the strategic value of IT outsourcing
article_text: Recent research also underscores the impact of AI on productivity: surveys indicate generative AI could boost BPO service productivity by over 50 % as organizations move from pilot to production-level deployments. At the same time, market forecasts show the agentic AI workforce- autonomous systems augmenting human work- growing at a compound annual rate near 44 % in the coming years, reflecting intensified automation across sectors.With this technological infusion comes a necessary shift in mindset. AI governance , ethical deployment, data security and performance measurement are now intrinsic to successful outsourcing relationships. Providers are increasingly judged not just on efficiency gains but on their ability to embed responsible AI practices and deliver measurable impact tied to business goals.India remains central to this evolution. Its talent depth, digital maturity and growing innovation ecosystems continue to attract global demand. Importantly, Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities are emerging as resilient, skilled delivery hubs, enabling distributed, hybrid service models that combine on-shore agility and global scale. Leadership choices will define how effectively the BPO industry adapts. Enterprises and their partners must invest in continuous workforce upskilling, hybrid human-AI operating models, and collaborative co-creation rather than transactional engagements. The most forward-looking CIOs and CTOs are already integrating AI strategically- not to displace human talent, but to augment decision-making, predictive insights and customer outcomes.The outsourcing sector is therefore not being displaced by AI and economic shifts; it is being fundamentally reimagined. Providers that succeed will be those that combine advanced technology with governance, human context and strategic alignment. For enterprises navigating complexity, the opportunity ahead lies in partnerships that deliver intelligence, agility and sustained strategic value -not mere execution.The author is Sapna Bhambani, SVP & Country Leader, TaskUs India.

title: Italy closes probe into DeepSeek after commitments to warn of AI 'hallucination' risks
article_text: Italy's antitrust authority has ‍ended an investigation into the Chinese AI system ⁠DeepSeek for allegedly failing to warn users that it may produce false information, ‌agreeing to ‌binding commitments as a condition for closing ‌the case.The Italian regulator, known as the AGCM, which also polices consumer rights, had launched the investigation last June.The decision was announced in the AGCM's regular weekly bulletin ‌published on ‍Monday.The commitments proposed by ‍Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence and Beijing ‌DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence, which jointly own and operate DeepSeek, consist of a package of measures aimed at improving disclosures about the risk of 'hallucinations' - situations in which, ‍based on a given input from a user, the AI ‍model ⁠generates one ⁠or more outputs containing inaccurate, misleading, or fabricated information."The commitments presented by DeepSeek make disclosures about the risk of hallucinations easier, more transparent, intelligible, and immediate," the AGCM bulletin said.>

title: Why data quality and authenticity will be essential for AI in 2025
article_text: Trust is your data currency: Data quality is one of the most important things we must grapple with, but it’s getting harder to prove. Efforts like the EU AI Act can help, but more is needed. The current focus is on how a model was created and trained, but we need to be able to signal whether a model can be trusted. An AI Trust Score acts as a filter through which all data should go - establishing provenance, lineage, and ultimately, overall trust. Data profiling markers will become important, particularly discoverability, accuracy, consumability, timeliness, security, and diversity. A common language for your data: The idea of a single data lakehouse that brings together the best of data lakes and warehouses while supporting multiple use cases has been around for a while. However, a lack of interoperability has restricted this vision to theory. Deploying open table formats, particularly Iceberg, is emerging as a modular format every vendor will accept, as it enables companies to organize their data in any storage, avoiding vendor lock-in. This, in turn, could help reduce costs, boost velocity, and improve governance. But more importantly, it brings interoperability that supports a single view of data. It creates a common language for the entire industry, which will help organizations demonstrate authenticity. Data and AI marketplaces become the place to trade your quality data: As demand for authentic, high-quality data skyrockets, private data increases in value. This further incentivizes making it a product. This is a concept that resonates. According to BARC, 82% of organizations have already implemented data-as-a-product as a concept or are planning to. Like any product, however, it needs a marketplace where it can be exchanged. Gartner estimates that, by 2028, 40% of enterprise AI asset purchases, including data and AI models, will occur via AI marketplaces. Vetted domain-specific, ethical, quality-controlled data will be worth gold. It’s a win-win for companies - you improve access to new, trusted sources, and set yourself up for a future of internally and externally gaining from the unique data you are producing. And the likelihood of that happening will only increase as businesses remove information from the public domain - another unintended consequence of the AI boom. Unauthenticated access is becoming restricted, with less high-quality content available without at least a registration. Publishers and authors are suing OpenAI , and closed platforms such as Medium and Substack are welcoming an influx of creators. YouTube has stopped regular users from being able to transcribe videos. In fact, an MIT-led research group estimated that 25% of data from the highest-quality sources that appear in three commonly used training sets have been removed.The result is an authenticity crisis that undermines the quality of LLMs and, ultimately, trust in the models. Some have indicated that it’s difficult to evolve the models further without copyrighted materials. In the hunt for authentic data to train the AI, your corporate data IP is next. As a result, data quality and authenticity will become highly valued - and the demand to prove provenance will soar. Generative AI has unleashed an unprecedented tsunami of hyper-realistic content, blurring the lines between authentic and artificial, which could erode information integrity to the point that the internet becomes unusable. To let this happen could be nothing short of catastrophic. Commerce would come to a standstill and global economies would falter.Model collapse occurs when an AI model stops being usable because it no longer produces useful and representative content. It’s imperative that organizations seek trust building and verification of sources, because it is the only way to combat the very real risk of model collapse. That’s why authentic output - based on real data produced by real people with real perspectives, rather than artificially generated ones - will be at a premium in the very near future.To cut through the ambiguity, businesses must build authenticity, deliver applied value, and accelerate into the agentic systems era.

title: AI In Arbitration: Use Cases And Considerations For 2026.
article_text: AI is no longer at the margins of legal practice: it is becoming central to how disputes are resolved, resources are allocated, and justice is delivered. AI in arbitration promises faster workflows, sharper insights, and more predictable outcomes. Yet its growing presence also raises critical questions about transparency, fairness, and human oversight. Common uses of AI for arbitration include research, document review, analysis, and drafting. As these tools are introduced, nuanced discussions about ethical use and governance within the arbitration community continue. Updated in September 2025, the Chartered Institute of Arbitrators (Ciarb) Guideline on the Use of AI in Arbitration provides a crucial reference point. The Guideline offers an up-to-date framework centred around procedural integrity, accountability, and trust— the unchanged principles at the core of arbitration. The use cases below illustrate how AI is already reshaping arbitration. They also reveal the need for balance between automation and human judgement and between efficiency and impartiality. For practitioners, each use case is not just a technological capability—it’s a call to consider how to align innovation with the core principles that make arbitration a trusted dispute resolution forum. For arbitrators, speech-to-text capabilities improve efficiency during hearings by allowing them to stay focused on the proceedings while automatically creating a reliable transcript for later reference. The current use and predicted growth of AI in arbitration must be paired with human legal expertise, lived experience, and evolving real-world knowledge. In arbitration, lawyers are selected based on skill and outcomes. Similarly, arbitrators are appointed based on trust and judgement. Arbitration is not a purely about analysing data and facts. It involves credibility assessments, contextual understanding, and ethical discernment. While AI can organise information and surface patterns, it can not weigh competing narratives or appreciate the broader consequences of a decision. Transparency is crucial when relying on AI-generated outputs to assess risk and prepare legal submissions. Legal professionals must be able to explain to clients—and, if necessary, to tribunals—how AI tools contributed to their strategy or conclusions. Understanding how AI tools arrive at summaries or recommendations helps practitioners ensure that they remain the ultimate decision-makers and do not unintentionally defer to opaque or flawed logic. Explainable outputs allow arbitrators to cross-check AI-assisted analysis against their own reasoning and legal judgement. Furthermore, well-reasoned arbitral awards could address some of the concerns around the “black box” problem. Guideline Section 7 encourages appropriate disclosure of AI use in arbitration—particularly when AI-generated content affects evidence, argumentation, and awards. And Section 9 promotes transparency over use of AI by arbitrators. For lawyers, this means clearly communicating when AI has contributed to a submission, shaped an argument, or analysed a body of evidence. Proper disclosure ensures that opposing parties and the tribunal can assess the content’s reliability and ensure validity and enforceability of an award. Such disclosures could also assist lawyers to comply with their ethical duties towards their clients and the tribunal. For arbitrators, disclosure may apply to any personal use of AI to aid decision-making. While internal tools may assist with research or synthesis, transparency about their role helps maintain confidence in award integrity. Arbitrators are selected to personally determine a dispute on the basis of their knowledge and skill; this is a personal mandate that any use of an AI tool should respect. Managing disclosure of AI use can reduce the risks associated with arbitrators using AI and also mitigate any trust issues around its use.2 Accuracy is non-negotiable and overreliance on unchecked outputs can lead to flawed arguments and reputational risk. Practitioners must verify AI-generated legal summaries, precedent suggestions, and procedural analyses. Reliability means never delegating core decision-making functions to machines. AI may assist in surfacing relevant facts or structuring information, but practitioners must retain control of legal reasoning and findings. This is of huge importance when it comes to tribunal decisions where the final award must reflect independent human judgement. AI systems are only as objective as the data they are trained on, and in many cases, that data reflects long-standing patterns of bias. In arbitration, this can affect everything from how arbitrators are selected to how evidence is analysed and presented. Guideline Section 2.4 highlights these risks and urges practitioners to actively assess AI systems for fairness and inclusivity and also raises the importance of taking responsibility for any output delivered. Bias mitigation means recognising the limitations of AI-generated outputs when analysing risk and interpreting case trends. Practitioners should work with vendors who are transparent about their training data and bias reduction techniques and should verify that outputs are balanced and representative. While adoption is growing, some concerns persist. The top three concerns according to the 2025 survey centre around errors and bias (51%), confidentiality and data protection (47%), and limited familiarity with AI (44%). These figures reflect a thoughtful and evolving conversation about how best to integrate AI into arbitral practice. As arbitration enters this next phase, the question is no longer whether we will use AI, but how. Will we implement it in a way that narrows access and reinforces bias? Or will we harness it to make arbitration more inclusive, informed, and resilient? The Ciarb Guideline challenges practitioners not just to adopt AI tools, but to do so thoughtfully, with a commitment to transparency, due process, and ethical integrity. The use cases explored in this article highlight what’s possible, but the Guideline offers a roadmap for balancing innovation with accountability, and efficiency with trust. Importantly, all arbitration practitioners should keep up to date with new AI developments. They must make sure they receive appropriate training to take advantage of AI tools whilst limiting their risks. It is, after all, a balancing act that everyone must master. The Ciarb Guideline itself clearly states that it was developed on the basis of the current state of AI and would be updated, as needed, in the future.

title: The Rise of Authentic Digital Trust in the Age of AI Content
article_text: AI-written posts, AI-edited videos, and AI-generated images are now normal online. The challenge is that “polished” no longer proves anything. If almost anyone can publish confident-looking content at scale, your credibility has to come from authentic proof. Startups and digital brands feel this. You are competing in feeds full of lookalike posts and recycled ideas. That forces one question: what makes someone trust you when they cannot tell what’s real and what’s fake? What replaced that old trust is lived experience. People want peer validation, not brand promises. They look for details that are difficult to invent. Compare “Our tool saves time” with “I stopped copying numbers into spreadsheets because the receipt scan finally handled my crumpled store receipts.” The second one has context and a specific outcome, so it feels real. There is also a simple psychological reason. You trust people over brands because people are more relatable. Plus, they seem to have something to lose. A person can lose their reputation for recommending a bad product. A brand can reframe, repackage, and keep going. When many independent voices point to the same pattern, your brain treats it like evidence. Press Box 🏀 Stay up to date on all things Illini sports. Delivered every Monday. This site is protected by reCAPTCHA and the Google Privacy Policy and Terms of Service apply. Subscribe Thank you for subscribing! AI can help you move faster. You can draft outlines, translate pages, test headlines, and summarize notes. Used carefully, it frees time for the human work that builds trust, like interviewing customers, checking facts, and adding grounded examples. But AI also makes it easy to publish content that looks certain while being shallow or wrong. It can flatten personality into a generic voice. It can repeat the same structure across hundreds of sites, which trains readers to distrust the pattern. It can even mimic a “personal” tone without any real lived experience behind it. So authenticity becomes harder to prove and more valuable. You are competing on believability, which pushes you toward transparent processes and signals that are difficult to fake at scale. Communities change how trust works. Instead of “listen to us,” trust becomes “watch how we behave together.” Communities influence decisions because they create shared standards. People learn what gets corrected, what gets challenged, and what stays useful over time. User participation becomes decentralized credibility. Someone asks a question, a few members answer, and another person adds a warning or a better method. That back-and-forth shows real use, not just claims, and it creates a trail of context that is hard to manufacture for long. So, let users share templates and fixes. Invite stories, then keep them intact, including small downsides. Those rough edges often make the content trustworthy. But short-term growth tactics can sometimes help if used well. Some teams use paid boosts or services like buy instagram reposts or buy facebook post likes to increase visibility early. The only trade-off is that attention is not trust. If the content behind the boost feels empty, extra reach just spreads doubt. A healthier balance is to treat promotion as the doorway, then earn credibility as the main pitch. Pair any boost with credible and authentic content. Show how it works. Share limits. Use real examples. Make it easy for people to verify you. Transparency is the baseline. If you use AI to draft, translate, or edit, do not pretend a machine-written story is a personal account. When something could mislead, label it. When an image is altered, say so. Trust frameworks will become more visible inside platforms. Expect more verification, more proof signals that show where media came from, and more tools that detect synthetic content. These systems will not solve everything, but they raise expectations for honest creators. Over the next decade, authenticity will define successful digital brands because it will be scarce. AI will make content cheap, but trust will stay expensive. The winners will be the brands that can prove they listen and connect their claims to real outcomes. Authenticity is not a feature you add once. It is a practice you repeat. You build it by being clear about what is real, inviting your audience into the process, and choosing long-term credibility even when quick wins look tempting.

title: AI video detection tools: Do they really work or are they flawed?
article_text: AI detection tools are increasingly used to identify deepfakes and synthetic media online, but recent testing suggests their reliability remains uncertain Artificial intelligence detection tools are designed to analyse videos and images to determine whether they are authentic or generated using AI systems. These tools typically examine hidden watermarks, digital artefacts, pixel-level inconsistencies and structural irregularities that may indicate synthetic manipulation. However, testing conducted by The New York Times found that while some AI-generated media could be identified, overall accuracy was inconsistent. In certain cases, the detectors successfully flagged manipulated content. In others, they failed to identify synthetic material or incorrectly suggested that authentic media might be altered. The results highlight a broader technological gap. While AI systems used to generate synthetic media are advancing rapidly, detection tools are still developing and often struggle to keep pace. Many detectors rely on patterns specific to known AI models, meaning that newer or modified systems can bypass existing detection mechanisms. As synthetic media becomes more sophisticated, the debate continues over whether detection technology can evolve quickly enough to maintain trust in digital content. For now, AI video detection tools serve as supplementary aids rather than conclusive proof of authenticity.

title: Fewer than 1% of HHS’s AI uses are ‘high impact.’ It stands out.
article_text: Out of the nearly 450 entries on the Department of Health and Human Services’ most recent AI use case inventory, just two fall into a category that would demand heightened risk management practices, raising questions about the agency’s governance process. “When we see an agency like HHS have a pretty significant jump in their overall total number of active use cases while simultaneously seeing no increase in the number of high-impact, I think we should be immediately questioning that,” Quinn Anex-Ries, a senior policy analyst at the Center for Democracy and Technology’s equity in civic technology team, told FedScoop. The high-impact designation for AI uses was first outlined in the Trump administration’s April 2025 memo to agencies on AI governance. It came as an evolution of labels created under former President Joe Biden to identify and manage deployments that pose potential risks. Those were known as rights- and safety-impacting uses. One of those entries, titled “Scholarship Insight,” would deploy generative AI to review essays submitted for the National Health Service Corps and Nurse Corps Scholarship. That AI would be trained to review those essays and “do the first cut at scoring.” Cody Venzke, a senior policy counsel in the ACLU’s National Political Advocacy Department who is focused on surveillance, privacy, and technology, pointed to longstanding concerns with agencies’ ability to make exceptions in their publications. “Every day, the Federal Government takes action and makes decisions that have consequential impacts on the public,” Trump’s OMB memo said. “If AI is used to perform such action, agencies must deploy trustworthy AI, ensuring that rapid AI innovation is not achieved at the expense of the American people or any violations of their trust.”

title: The End of Voluntary Ethics: Pacific AI’s 2025 AI Policy Year in Review Details the Global Shift to Enforceable AI Law
article_text: With 30+ nations and 15+ U.S. states passing new AI laws, and a 200% surge in mandatory incident reporting, AI governance has turned into an operational and fiduciary imperative LEWES, Del., Jan. 13, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Pacific AI, the healthcare AI governance company, today published its 2025 AI Policy Year in Review, a compilation of its quarterly AI governance policy release notes. The report summarizes how sweeping regulatory, legislative, and standards-based developments reshaped AI compliance across the globe over the last year, and how organizations can respond with confidence. Throughout 2025, AI regulation accelerated at a breakneck pace. Governments moved from high-level principles to enforceable obligations, particularly in healthcare, generative AI, transparency, and accountability. Pacific AI’s quarterly policy updates translate these fragmented requirements into a single, actionable, free governance framework used by enterprises worldwide. “We’re at an inflection point in which AI governance has shifted from theoretical to operational,” said David Talby, CEO, Pacific AI. “Our policy release notes exist so organizations don’t have to track hundreds of laws on their own. We do the hard work of unifying them into a policy suite teams can actually implement.” State-Level Crackdown: 15+ U.S. states enacted landmark laws specifically targeting AI transparency and decision-making in healthcare, including Arizona, California, and Texas. Operational Burden Spike: Mandatory Incident Reporting requirements increased by 200% year-over-year, requiring organizations to report AI failures or biases in as little as 24 to 48 hours. Clinical Specialization: Pacific AI unified 22+ dedicated healthcare AI frameworks including CHAI, FUTURE-AI, and WHO ethics guidelines into a single operational standard. Simplifying the Patchwork: Pacific AI’s unified policy suite now navigates 250+ laws, regulations, and industry standards, turning a fragmented legal landscape into a clear compliance roadmap, and keeps it current.

title: Protos Security Marks 20 Years with Launch of AI-Driven Security Operations Platform at ISC West 2026
article_text: LAS VEGAS, March 3, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- As security threats grow more complex and organizations demand greater transparency and accountability from their providers, Protos Security will debut a new AI-powered operational agent at ISC West 2026, designed to bring real-time insight and automation directly into guarding and off-duty programs. Protos will exhibit at booth #24131 at the Venetian Expo in Las Vegas. "Software should amplify the expertise of security professionals, not complicate it," said Cameron Tabor, Senior Vice President of Software Engineering at Protos. "Our AI-powered agent acts as a force multiplier. It allows users to quickly surface critical insights across their entire footprint — from open posts to fulfillment patterns — and take action without digging through layers of data. That real-time visibility strengthens accountability and performance across every location." Protos currently maintains 98% customer retention and supports its national footprint through a network of more than 2,800 vendor branch locations and 60,000 off-duty law enforcement professionals. The company's data-validated billing model delivers to-the-penny accuracy, reinforcing its commitment to transparency and operational discipline. Founded in 2006 to modernize a traditionally fragmented security industry, Protos built a distributed, vendor-managed model long before digital transformation became an industry mandate. Rather than operating as a monolithic guarding firm, Protos developed a software-enabled platform designed to deliver greater flexibility, visibility and accountability at scale.

title: 'Suffering distancing, hallucinations': AI & ChatGPT's problems and pitfalls
article_text: Bali (Indonesia), Aug 29 (IANS) It is not new technology, but Artificial Intelligence (AI) is now raising concerns with the advent of generative AI tools like ChatGPT, which may have significant repercussions across the cyber landscape, as they foster phenomenon like “suffering distancing syndrome”, “responsibility delegation”, and “AI hallucination” for those simply using it to find or validate information, says a senior Kaspersky Labs cyberthreat expert. “With the rise of generative AI, we have seen a breakthrough of technology that can synthesise content very similar to what humans do: from images to sound, deepfake videos, and even text-based conversations indistinguishable from human peers… Kamluk stressed that AI “needs to be taken with a grain of salt and that people need to be aware of AI hallucination, as it is a phenomena present among all LLMs, including ChatGPT” and there is no cure for it. He clarified that he, in fact, has not written any books and that this phenomenon, called hallucinations, is what people need to be aware of. “I do have hope in humanity and the intelligence of mankind that it will be used to improve our lives…,” he said, listing some ways that people and businesses can reap the benefits, including restricting anonymous access to real intelligent systems, having proper regulations, including for detecting AI-developed content, licensing AI developers and especially, awareness among people.

title: AI in the dock: India Supreme Court warns of misconduct
article_text: “At the outset, we must declare that a decision based on such non-existent and fake alleged judgments is not an error in the decision making. It would be a misconduct and legal consequences shall follow. It is compelling that we examine this issue in more detail,” it added, according to PTI.

title: It's too easy to make AI chatbots lie about health information, study finds
